Noting that $\sqrt{\omega} \geq \sqrt{\omega(1-\omega)}$ , we then have that this latter simplified condition is ![](_page_5_Figure_1.jpeg) Fig. 1. Theoretical comparison between PZF-SSC and ZF-SSC: The functions $f_{\rm PZF}(\omega)$ and $f_{\rm ZF}(\omega)$ defined in (34) and (36) respectively are plotted for $\alpha=0.9, \beta=0.01, \epsilon=0.001$ . satisfied if the stronger condition $\alpha > (1+\sqrt{2})\sqrt{\omega}$ is true. In turn, this implies that $$\text{ZF-SSC}: \ \frac{m}{D} < \frac{1}{(1+\sqrt{2})^2} \frac{\log(\rho)}{16d} = \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{d}\right). \tag{33}$$ In other words, both PZF-SSC and ZF-SSC give subspace preserving solutions as long as the ratio of missing entries scales as $^8$ 1/d. On the other hand, the multiplying constant associated to PZF-SSC is about 3 times larger, a significant difference. Alternatively (and more rigorously), defining $$f_{\text{PZF}}(\omega) := \alpha - \sqrt{2\omega} - \beta\sqrt{1-\omega} - (1+\beta)\sqrt{\epsilon + \beta/3}, \tag{34}$$ the PZF Theorem 4 asks that $$f_{\rm PZF}(\omega) > 0, \tag{35}$$ while the ZF Theorem 6 asks that $$f_{\rm ZF}(\omega) := -\sqrt{\epsilon + \beta/3} (\sqrt{\omega} + \sqrt{1 - \omega} + \sqrt{\epsilon + \beta/3}) - \sqrt{\omega(1 - \omega)} + f_{\rm PZF}(\omega) > 0.$$ (36) To appreciate the difference between the two functions $f_{\rm PZF}(\omega)$ and $f_{\rm ZF}(\omega)$ , we plot them in Fig. 19, which suggests the superiority of PZF-SSC. [Chunk 2] In this appendix we collect some technical elements from convex geometry and high-dimensional probability theory, that are used extensively in the technical development of the paper. Proposition 1 is known to experts, yet being unaware of a direct proof in the literature, we provide one. **Definition 3.** [Inradius, Circumradius, Polar Set] Let $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times L}$ be a set of L points of $\mathbb{R}^D$ . We denote by $r(\mathbf{Y})$ the radius of the largest Euclidean ball of $\mathrm{Span}(\mathbf{Y})$ that is contained in the convex hull of the points $\mathbf{Y}$ ; we refer to $r(\mathbf{Y})$ as the releative inradius. Dually, we denote by $\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{Y})$ the radius of the smallest Euclidean ball of $\mathrm{Span}(\mathbf{Y})$ that contains $\mathbf{Y}$ ; we refer to $\mathcal{R}(\mathbf{Y})$ as the relative circumradius. The polar set $\mathbf{Y}^\circ$ of $\mathbf{Y}$ is the convex subset of $\mathbb{R}^D$ $$\boldsymbol{Y}^{\circ} := \left\{ \boldsymbol{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{D} : \ \boldsymbol{y}^{\top} \boldsymbol{v} \leq 1, \, \forall \boldsymbol{y} \in \boldsymbol{Y} \right\}.$$ (130) **Lemma 2** (§1.2 in [13]). Let $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times L}$ be a set of L points of $\mathbb{R}^D$ . Then $$\mathcal{R}((\mathbf{Y})^{\circ} \cap \operatorname{Span}(\mathbf{Y})) = 1/r(\mathbf{Y}). \tag{131}$$ **Proposition 1.** For $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times L}$ a set of L points of $\mathbb{R}^D$ , $$r(\boldsymbol{Y}) = \min_{\boldsymbol{v}: \|\boldsymbol{v}\|_2 = 1} \|\boldsymbol{Y}^{\top} \boldsymbol{v}\|_{\infty}.$$ (132) Proof. From Lemma 2 we have that $$r(\mathbf{Y}) = 1/\mathcal{R}((\mathbf{Y})^{\circ} \cap \operatorname{Span}(\mathbf{Y}))$$ (133) $$=1/\max_{\boldsymbol{v}\in(\pm\boldsymbol{Y})^{\circ}\cap\mathrm{Span}(\boldsymbol{Y})}\|\boldsymbol{v}\|_{2} \tag{134}$$ $$= 1 / \max_{\boldsymbol{v} \in \operatorname{Span}(\boldsymbol{Y}) : \|\boldsymbol{Y}^{\top}\boldsymbol{v}\|_{\infty} \le 1} \|\boldsymbol{v}\|_{2}$$ (135) $$= 1 / \max_{\boldsymbol{v} \in \operatorname{Span}(\boldsymbol{Y}) : \|\boldsymbol{Y}^{\top} \boldsymbol{v}\|_{\infty} = 1} \|\boldsymbol{v}\|_{2}$$ (136) $$= \min_{\boldsymbol{v}: \|\boldsymbol{v}\|_{\alpha} = 1} \|\boldsymbol{Y}^{\top} \boldsymbol{v}\|_{\infty}, \tag{137}$$ where in the last equality we used that $$\sup_{g(v)=1} f(v) = \sup_{g\left(\frac{v}{f(v)}\right) = \frac{1}{f(v)}} f(v) = 1/\inf_{f(u)=1} g(u), \tag{138}$$ for any 1-homogeneous functions $f, g : \mathbb{R}^D \to \mathbb{R}$ . **Lemma 3** ([2], [22]). Let Y be a set of L random points uniformly distributed on $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ . Then for $\rho := L/d$ larger than a constant, we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[r(\boldsymbol{Y}) \le \sqrt{\frac{\log(\rho)}{16d}}\right] \le \exp\left(-\sqrt{\rho}d\right). \tag{139}$$ **Lemma 4** (Follows from Theorem 1.5 in [28]). Let $x, \hat{v}$ be independent random vectors on $\mathbb{S}^{D-1}$ , with x uniformly distributed. Then for every $\epsilon > 0$ we have $$\mathbb{P}\Big[|\boldsymbol{x}^{\top}\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}| \ge \epsilon\Big] \le 2\exp\left(-D\epsilon^2/2\right). \tag{140}$$ **Lemma 5** (Follows from Lemma 5.3.2 in [29]). [Question] What implications can be drawn from the comparison of PZF-SSC and ZF-SSC regarding the conditions under which subspace preserving solutions are achieved, and how does the difference in the multiplying constant affect the performance of these methods?

The comparison between PZF-SSC and ZF-SSC indicates that both methods provide subspace preserving solutions as long as the ratio of missing entries scales as \( \frac{1}{d} \). However, the multiplying constant associated with PZF-SSC is approximately three times larger than that of ZF-SSC, which signifies a significant difference in performance. This suggests that while both methods can achieve similar outcomes under the specified conditions, PZF-SSC may require more stringent conditions or a larger dataset to perform comparably to ZF-SSC due to its larger constant.

[Chunk 2]
We split our handcrafted lexicon in 85% training set, 5% validation and 10% test set. Intrinsic evaluation on unseen test set our G2P model achieved word error rate (WER) of 36%. The same G2P model trained on CMUdict has WER of 28.61% (Yao et al., 2015). The low word error rate of CMUdict can be attributed to its large size. Another reason for our comparatively higher WER may be that only about 11% of the words in our corpus have diacritics. As a result, a good performance would require overcoming the problem of automatic diacritization which gets harder while processing a list of isolated words without any context.  
To perform extrinsic evaluation of the performance of lexicon tool on speech recognition task, we trained a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) based speech recognition system on phonetically rich Urdu speech corpus<sup>18</sup> (Raza et al., 2009) and spontaneous speech corpus (Raza et al., 2010) using CMUSphinx<sup>19</sup> speech recognition toolkit. The combined data from both corpora contains 3,974 utterances spanning over 179 minutes of speech, out of which 157 minutes (3,174 utterances) were used for training and 22 minutes (800 utterances) for testing. A tri-  
<sup>17</sup> https://github.com/cmusphinx/g2p-seq2seq  
<sup>18</sup> http://csalt.itu.edu.pk/PRUSCorpus/index.html  
<sup>19</sup> https://cmusphinx.github.io/  
gram language model using the training data transcripts was applied during decoding. By using lexicon generated through lexicon tool, we obtained a word error rate (~19%) that approaches the rate achieved using a fully handcrafted expert lexicon. We used the same train/test split as used by Raza et al. (2010) and thus results are directly comparable.

[Question]
How does the performance evaluation of the G2P model, which achieved a word error rate (WER) of 36% on the test set, relate to the phonemes included in the training lexicon of approximately 46K words that caters for 64 phonemes in Urdu?

The performance evaluation of the G2P model, which achieved a word error rate (WER) of 36% on the test set, is influenced by the phonemes included in the training lexicon. The lexicon, consisting of approximately 46K words, caters to 64 phonemes from the Urdu phonetic inventory. However, it is noted that the model's performance may be affected by the fact that only about 11% of the words in the corpus have diacritics, which complicates the automatic diacritization process. This challenge is exacerbated when processing isolated words without context, potentially leading to a higher WER. The inclusion of 64 phonemes indicates a comprehensive approach to capturing the phonetic diversity of Urdu, but the relatively high WER suggests that further improvements, such as addressing diacritization issues, are necessary for better performance.

To compare the performance of DD-RNN with other standard RNN architectures, we trained the following three models to generate the split predictions on our benchmark dataset: (i) unidirectional encoder and decoder without attention (RNN), (ii) bi-directional encoder and decoder without attention (B-RNN), and (iii) bi-directional encoder and decoder with attention (B-RNN-A)

#### 4.3 Comparison with similar works  
(Reddy et al., 2018) propose a *seq2seq* model with attention to tackle the Sandhi problem. Their model is similar to B-RNN-A and is outperformed by our proposed DD-RNN by 6.47%. We also ˜ compared our proposed DD-RNN with a unidirectional LSTM with a depth of 4 (Chen et al., 2015b) (LSTM-4) and a Gated Recursive Neural Network with a depth of 5 (Chen et al., 2015a) (GRNN-5). These models were used to get state of the art results for Chinese word segmentation and their source code is made available online.<sup>4</sup> Since these models can only predict the location(s) of the split(s) and cannot generate the split words themselves, we used the location prediction accuracy as the metric. Conversely, we trained the DD-RNN for the Chinese word segmentation task to test the generalizability of the model. Since there are no morphological changes during segmentation in Chinese, the character decoder is redundant and the model collapses to simple *seq2seq*. To summarize, we have used our benchmark dataset to compare the DD-RNN model with existing publicly available Sandhi splitting tools, other RNN architectures and models used for Chinese word segmentation task. Among the existing tools, the INRIA splitter gives the highest split prediction accuracy of 59.9%. Among the standard RNN architectures, B-RNN-A performs the best with a split prediction accuracy of 69.3%. LSTM-4 performs the best among the Chinese word segmentation models with a location prediction accuracy of 70.2%. DD-RNN outperforms all the models both in location and split predictions with 95% and 79.5% accuracies, respectively.

2. **Location Decoder**: DD-RNN includes a location decoder, which enhances its ability to predict split locations more accurately.

3. **Training on Benchmark Dataset**: DD-RNN was trained on a benchmark dataset, allowing it to achieve higher accuracy metrics compared to other models.

4. **Performance Metrics**: DD-RNN achieved an accuracy of 79.5% for split predictions, which is significantly higher than B-RNN-A and other architectures, indicating its effectiveness in this task.

Overall, these factors contribute to DD-RNN's ability to outperform B-RNN-A and other RNN architectures in both location and split predictions.

The architecture of the DD-RNN is shown in Figure 2. We used a character embedding size of 128. The bi-directional encoder and the two decoders are 2 layers deep with 512 LSTM units in each layer. A dropout layer with p = 0.3 is applied after each LSTM layer. The entire network is implemented in Torch <sup>2</sup> .  
Of the 71, 747 words in our benchmark dataset, we randomly sampled 80% of the data for training our models. The remaining 20% was used for testing. We used stochastic gradient descent for optimizing the model parameters with an initial learning rate of 1.0. The learning rate was decayed by a factor of 0.5 if the validation perplexity did not improve after an epoch. We used a batch size of 64 and trained the network for 10 epochs on four Tesla K80 GPUs. This setup remains the same for all the experiments we conduct.

[Question]
What design decisions regarding the architecture and training process of the DD-RNN are made in this section, and how do these decisions contribute to the overall implementation of the model?

The design decisions regarding the architecture of the DD-RNN include using a character embedding size of 128, a bi-directional encoder, and two decoders that are 2 layers deep with 512 LSTM units in each layer. A dropout layer with a probability of 0.3 is applied after each LSTM layer to prevent overfitting.

For the training process, 80% of the benchmark dataset (71,747 words) was randomly sampled for training, while the remaining 20% was used for testing. Stochastic gradient descent was chosen for optimizing model parameters, starting with an initial learning rate of 1.0, which is decayed by a factor of 0.5 if validation perplexity does not improve after an epoch. A batch size of 64 was used, and the network was trained for 10 epochs on four Tesla K80 GPUs.

These decisions contribute to the overall implementation of the model by ensuring a robust architecture capable of learning complex patterns while maintaining generalization through dropout. The training strategy, including the learning rate decay and batch size, is designed to optimize performance and efficiency during the training process.

[Context]
[Chunk 1]
The critical part of learning to split compound words is to correctly identify the location(s) of the split(s). Therefore, we added a two decoders to our bi-directional encoder-decoder model: (i) *location decoder* which learns to predict the split locations and (ii) *character decoder* which generates the split words. A compound word is fed into the encoder character by character. Each character's embedding x<sup>i</sup> is passed to the encoders LSTM units. There are two LSTM layers which encode the word, one in forward direction and the other backward. The encoded context vector e<sup>i</sup> is then passed to a global attention layer.  
In the first phase of training, only the *location decoder* is trained and the *character decoder* is frozen. The character embeddings are learned from scratch in this phase along with the attention weights and other parameters. Here, the model learns to identify the split locations. For example, if the inputs are the embeddings for the compound word *protsahah ¯ .* , the location decoder will generate a binary vector [0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0] which indicates that the split occurs between the third and fourth characters. In the second phase, the *location decoder* is frozen and the *character decoder* is trained. The encoder and attention weights are allowed to be fine-tuned. This decoder learns the underlying rules of Sandhi splitting. Since the attention layer is already pre-trained to identify potential split locations in the previous phase, the *character decoder* can use this context and learn to split the words more accurately. For example, for the same input word *protsahah ¯ .* , the *character decoder* will generate [p, r, a, +, u, t, s, a, h, a, h ¯ . ] as the output. Here the character *o* is split into two characters *a* and *u*.  
In both the training phases, we use negative log likelihood as the loss function. Let X be the sequence of the input compound word's characters and Y be the binary vector which indicates the location of the split(s) in the first phase and the true target sequence of characters which form the split words in the second phase. If Y = y1, y2, ..., yn, then the loss function is defined as:  
$$loss = -\sum_{i=1}^{|Y|} \log P(y_i|y_{i-1}, \dots, y_1, X)$$

Figure 2: The bi-directional encoder and decoders with attention

[Question]
What are the two phases of training in the DD-RNN model, and how does the freezing of decoders in each phase contribute to the model's ability to learn Sandhi splitting?

The two phases of training in the DD-RNN model are:

1. **First Phase**: In this phase, only the *location decoder* is trained while the *character decoder* is frozen. The model learns to identify the split locations of the compound words. Character embeddings, attention weights, and other parameters are learned from scratch. For example, for the input *protsahah ¯.*, the location decoder generates a binary vector indicating where the split occurs.

2. **Second Phase**: Here, the *location decoder* is frozen, and the *character decoder* is trained. The encoder and attention weights are fine-tuned. This phase allows the character decoder to learn the underlying rules of Sandhi splitting using the context provided by the pre-trained attention layer, which has already identified potential split locations. For the same input word, the character decoder generates the split output.

The freezing of decoders in each phase allows the model to focus on learning specific tasks: identifying split locations in the first phase and generating split words in the second phase, thereby enhancing the model's ability to learn Sandhi splitting effectively.

[Question] What limitations does the current Kazakh Language Corpus impose on phoneme recognition and language identification, and what steps are necessary to address these limitations?

[Chunk 2] Akzharkyn Izbassarova, Aidana Irmanova and Alex Pappachen James School of Engineering, Nazarbayev University, Astana www.biomicrosystems.info/alex Email: apj@ieee.org *Abstract*—Effective presentation skills can help to succeed in business, career and academy. This paper presents the design of speech assessment during the oral presentation and the algorithm for speech evaluation based on criteria of optimal intonation. As the pace of the speech and its optimal intonation varies from language to language, developing an automatic identification of language during the presentation is required. Proposed algorithm was tested with presentations delivered in Kazakh language. For testing purposes the features of Kazakh phonemes were extracted using MFCC and PLP methods and created a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) [\[5\]](#page-4-0), [\[5\]](#page-4-0) of Kazakh phonemes. Kazakh vowel formants were defined and the correlation between the deviation rate in fundamental frequency and the liveliness of the speech to evaluate intonation of the presentation was analyzed. It was established that the threshold value between monotone and dynamic speech is 0.16 and the error for intonation evaluation is 19%. *Index Terms*—MFCC, PLP, presentations, speech, images, recognition [Question] How do the MFCC and PLP methods used for feature extraction in the algorithm for speech evaluation relate to the creation of the Hidden Markov Model (HMM) for Kazakh phonemes as referenced in the literature?

The MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) and PLP (Perceptual Linear Prediction) methods are utilized for feature extraction of Kazakh phonemes in the algorithm for speech evaluation. These methods help in capturing the essential characteristics of the speech signal, which are crucial for the subsequent creation of the Hidden Markov Model (HMM). The HMM is then developed using the extracted features from the Kazakh phonemes, allowing for effective modeling of the speech patterns necessary for evaluating intonation during presentations. Thus, the relationship is that MFCC and PLP provide the foundational data needed to construct the HMM, which is integral to the speech assessment process.

[Chunk 2]
To conclude, in this paper we present the system that can be used to evaluate presentation skills of the speaker based on the intonation of the voice. To test the proposed design we used data in kazakh language which consequently led to consideration of language identification system. As language identification and speech recognition is a relatively new field for Kazakh language processing field, we believe that the development of such system could be useful for the further popularization of Kazakh language and realization of different projects that builds up on top of the Kazakh speech recognition systems.  
Future works cover the development of the Kazakh language corpus with the analysis and labeling up to phoneme level. After that, the language model for the Kazakh language can be developed. Finally, the larger database of the presentations in the Kazakh language should be created to analyze the presentation styles in the Kazakh language as well as to conduct a test and design an intonation evaluator.

[Question]
What implications does the development of a Kazakh language corpus and intonation evaluator have for the future of Kazakh language processing and its projects?

The development of a Kazakh language corpus and intonation evaluator has significant implications for the future of Kazakh language processing. It could facilitate the popularization of the Kazakh language and support various projects built on Kazakh speech recognition systems. Additionally, creating a comprehensive language corpus allows for the development of a language model for Kazakh, which can enhance the accuracy and effectiveness of language identification and speech recognition technologies. This groundwork will enable further analysis of presentation styles in the Kazakh language and improve the evaluation of presentation skills based on intonation.

First, the features corresponding to the Kazakh phonemes are extracted. Then the model for language recognition is developed based on Hidden Markov Model (HMM). MATLAB is used to create a HMM for Kazakh phonemes. The block diagram in Fig. [2](#page-1-0) illustrates the algorithm used in the code. The program should be able to evaluate the intonation and tempo of the speech. It is assumed that there is a direct correlation between the deviation rate in fundamental frequency and the liveliness of the speech. Thus, we need to conduct the ![](_page_1_Figure_0.jpeg) Figure 1. Flow chart for speech evaluation ![](_page_1_Figure_2.jpeg) Figure 2. Block diagram for phone recognition pitch analysis to identify whether the proposed hypothesis is true. The pitch variation quotient derived from pitch contour of the audio files, where pitch variation quotient is a ratio of standard deviation of the pitch to its mean should be found. In order to identify the variation of pitch during presentations, the database of the presentations given in Kazakh language is created. This database consists of five presentations with ten-minute duration for each presentation. It is obtained by taking a video of students class presentations giving during "Kazakh Music History" and "History of Kazakhstan" courses at Nazarbayev University. For the simplicity of the analysis, presentations are divided into one-minute long audio files converted to WAV format. As a result, we obtain 32 audio files where seven presentations are with male voices and the rest by female. By using WaveSurfer program, the pitch value is found for each 7.5 ms of the speech. Two different sampling frequency values are tested to identify which sampling rate should be applied to obtain better results. 16 kHz and 44.1 kHz sampling frequency values are available in WaveSurfer. Thus, pitch is measured at these sampling rates. Then the mean and standard deviation of the pitch corresponding to each audio file is obtained. After that, a pitch variation quotient calculated. In order to obtain the pitch variation quotient we divide the standard deviation of the pitch to its mean. Finally, the results of the pitch variation quotient should be compared to the results of a perception test. The same speech files used for pitch extraction are used to conduct a test on how people perceive the speech regarding intonation. The purpose of this test is to identify the correlation between how people evaluate the presentation and the value of the pitch variation quotient. Since the paper aims to evaluate the presentation skills based on criteria such as intonation and tempo of the speech and give feedback to the users, the ability of the program to assess should be consistent with that how would professionals and general audience evaluate the presentation. Thus, we will ask students and professors to participate in this test. They will listen to a speech from presentations and categorize the speech into "monotone" or "emotionless" and "dynamic" or "lively". Since the intonation during the presentation is not always constant, the speech will be divided into small segments so the participants will give feedback for each speech segment. They should give marks for each presentations based on the intonation of the speakers. A marking system is a following: 1- monotone, 2- middle and 3-dynamic. After that, all results will be analyzed and the average mark for each presentation will be calculated. These average marks are compared with the results of the pitch variation quotient. #### III. For any $\theta > 0$ and any $n \ge 1$ , we have that $$\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{F}, \theta, n) \le \left(\frac{2en}{\theta}\right)^{fat_{\theta}(\mathcal{F})}.$$ (19) In all of the proofs we use the following technical lemma about the meta-algorithm. **Lemma 3.** Irrespectively of the subroutine used by the meta-algorithm, for any $\alpha \in [0,1]$ and $\beta \in [0,\beta/4]$ , we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[\sup_{h\in\mathcal{H}}\left|\frac{1}{s_{I_{n},n}}\sum_{t\in C_{I_{n},n}}(\ell(h,\mathbf{z}_{t})-R_{t-1}(h))\right|>\alpha\wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ $$\leq \frac{2k\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}),\beta,n)}{(\alpha-4\beta)^{2}}e^{-\frac{1}{2}m(\alpha-4\beta)^{2}}.$$ (20) Moreover, for any $\alpha \in [0,1]$ with $g_{I_n,i} = \ell(h_{I_n,i-1}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}) - R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i-1})$ $$\mathbb{P}\left[\left|\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}g_{I_n,i}\right|>\alpha\wedge E_{k,m}\right]\leq \frac{2k}{\alpha^2}e^{-\frac{1}{2}m\alpha^2}.$$ *Proof.* Introduce events $A_i = \{I_n = i\}$ for $i = 1, \ldots, k$ and $B_{i,j} = \{s_{j,n} = i\}$ (we suppress the dependence on n to increase readability). Observe that $E_{k,m} = \{\cup_{i \geq 1} A_i\} \land \{\cup_{i \geq m} B_{i,I_n}\}$ . Denoting $\Lambda(j) = \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\frac{1}{s_{i,n}} \sum_{t \in C_{i,n}} (\ell(h, \mathbf{z}_t) - R_{t-1}(h))|$ , we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(I_n) > \alpha \wedge E_{k,m}\right] \tag{21}$$ $$\leq \sum_{j \in \text{supp}(I_n)} \mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge \left\{ \cup_{i \geq m} B_{i,j} \right\} \right]. \tag{22}$$ Each of the last probabilities can be bounded using a union bound. $$\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge \{\cup_{i \ge m} B_{i,j}\}\right] \le \sum_{i > m} \mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge B_{i,j}\right]. \tag{23}$$ Using Lemma 4 from [33], we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge B_{i,j}\right] \le 2\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}), \beta, n)e^{-\frac{1}{2}i(\alpha - 4\beta)^{2}}.$$ (24) Summing the probabilities, we obtain the first statement of the lemma. For the second part of the lemma, denote $\Lambda(j) = |\frac{1}{s_{j,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{j,n}} (\ell(h_{j,i-1},\mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i}}) - R_{t_{j,i}-1}(h_{j,i-1}))|$ and, using the same decomposition as above, we have to bound $\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge B_{i,j}\right]$ . Observe that each $h_{j,i}$ is adapted to the filtration generated by $\{\mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i}}\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ , hence, $\ell(h_{j,i},\mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i-1}}) - R_{t_{j,i-1}}(h_{j,i-1})$ behaves like a martingale difference sequence. However, there is a technical difficulty in the fact that the indices $t_{j,i}$ are in fact stopping times. To get around it, observe that we can write $\Lambda(j)$ as a sum over all the data with the adapted weights. Set $w_t$ to 1 if we updated the algorithm j at step t and to 0 otherwise. Correspondingly, define $h_t$ as the last chosen hypothesis by the j-th algorithm. This way, both $w_t$ and $h_t$ are adapted to the original process. Then $$\sum_{i=1}^{s_{j,n}} (\ell(h_{j,i-1}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i}}) - R_{t_{j,i}-1}(h_{j,i-1}))$$ (25) $$= \sum_{t=1}^{n} w_t (\ell(\bar{h}_t, \mathbf{z}_t) - R_{t-1}(\bar{h}_t)).$$ (26) At this point we can again use Lemma 4 from [33] and get the second statement of the lemma. $\Box$ *Proof of Lemma 1.* The lower bound comes from the fact that MACRO constructs an $\varepsilon$ -covering. For the upper bound, observe that a new subroutine is started if and only if its associated conditional distribution differs by more than $\varepsilon$ from the ones of all previously created subroutines. Therefore, the set of conditional distribution associated with subroutines form an $\varepsilon$ -separated set with respect to $M_{i,j}$ 's (no two elements are closer than $\varepsilon$ to each other). The maximal size of such a set is at most the *covering number* of half the distance. *Proof of Theorem 1.* We start by the usual argument for the empirical risk minimization that allows us to focus on the uniform deviations. $$R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) \le 2 \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |R_n(h) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)|. \tag{27}$$ Denoting by R¯ n(h, In) = 1 sIn,n P t∈CIn,n Rt−1(h), we can upper bound the last term. $$\sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |R_n(h) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{28}$$ $$\leq \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |R_n(h) - \bar{R}_n(h, I_n)| + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{29}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i \in C_{I_n,n}} d_{i,n+1} + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{30}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i \in C_{I_n,n}} M_{i,n+1} + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{31}$$ $$\leq 2\varepsilon + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)|, \tag{32}$$ where the last bound follows from the way the meta-algorithm chooses In. Hence, we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) > \alpha + 2\varepsilon\right]$$ (33) $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[\sup_{h\in\mathcal{H}}|\bar{R}_n(h,I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h,I_n)| > \alpha\right]. \tag{34}$$ The last probability can be bounded using Lemma [3](#page-10-0) giving us the statement of the theorem. *Proof of Theorem 2.* Note that by the way In is chosen, we get for any h ∈ H that $$R_n(h) - R_{t_{I_n,i-1}}(h) \le 2\varepsilon. \tag{35}$$ Therefore, by using the convexity of the loss $$R_n(h_n) \le \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_n(h_{I_n,i})$$ (36) $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) + 2\varepsilon. \tag{37}$$ Similarly, for any fixed h $$R_n(h) \ge \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h) - 2\varepsilon.$$ (38) Therefore, $$R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) \tag{39}$$ $$\leq 4\varepsilon + \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i})$$ (40) $$-\inf_{h\in\mathcal{H}}\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h). \tag{41}$$ We split the last difference into the following three terms and deal with them separately. $$T_1 = \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} (R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) - \ell(h_{I_n,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}))$$ (42) $$T_2 = \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h_{I_n,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})$$ (43) $$T_{3} = \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_{n},n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_{n},i}}) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{s_{I_{n},n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_{n},n}} R_{t_{I_{n},i}-1}(h).$$ (44) The first term can be bounded using Lemma 3. $T_2$ is in fact just $W_{I_n,n}$ . For $T_3$ observe that $$\inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h) \ge \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})$$ (45) $$+\inf_{h\in\mathcal{H}}\left(\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}(R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h)-\ell(h,\mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})).$$ (46) Therefore, $T_3$ is bounded by $\tilde{T}_3$ : $$\tilde{T}_{3} = \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \left( \frac{1}{s_{I_{n},n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_{n},n}} (\ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_{n},i}}) - R_{t_{I_{n},i}-1}(h)).$$ (47) Combining everything together, $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) - \inf_h R_n(h) > \alpha + 4\varepsilon + W_{I_n,n}\right]$$ (48) $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[T_1 + \tilde{T}_3 > \alpha \wedge E_{k,m}\right] + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^c\right] \tag{49}$$ $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[T_1 > \alpha/2 \wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ $$+ \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{T}_3 > \alpha/2 \wedge E_{k,m}\right] + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^c\right]$$ (50) The both terms in the last line can be bounded using Lemma 3 giving us the statement of the theorem. $\Box$ Online-to-batch conversion for non-convex losses. Here we describe the modification of the online-to-batch conversion method of [9]. As the original method was designed to work for i.i.d. data, we need to extend it to stochastic processes. The general idea is to assign a score to each of $h_{j,i}$ and choose the one with the lowest score. For a given confidence $\delta > 0$ , the score of $h_{j,i}$ is computed as $$u_n(j,i) = \tilde{R}_n(j,i) + c_{j,\delta}(s_{j,n} - i).$$ (51) where $$\tilde{R}_n(j,i) = \frac{1}{s_{j,n} - i} \sum_{k=i+1}^{s_{j,n}} \ell(h_{j,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{j,k}}) \quad \text{and} \quad c_{j,\delta}(t) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2(t+1)} \log \frac{s_{j,n}^3(s_{j,n}+1)}{\delta}}. \quad (52)$$ Setting $J_n = \operatorname{argmin}_{1 \leq i \leq s_{I_n,n}} u_n(I_n, i)$ , MACRO's output is $h_n = h_{I_n, J_n}$ . The following lemma is analog of Lemma 3 from [9] proved for the case of dependent data and the conditional risk. **Lemma 4.** For the setting of Theorem 3, let $$v(j,i) = R_n(h_{j,i}) + 2c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n} - i).$$ (53) Then we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge E_{k,m}\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{m}.$$ (54) *Proof.* Introduce events $A_r = \{|\sup(I_n)| \le k \land s_{I_n,n} = r\}$ . Using a union bound, we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ (55) $$\leq \sum_{r>m} \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \leq i \leq s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{56}$$ Therefore, we will focus on the last probabilities. Let $J_n^\star = \operatorname{argmin}_{1 \leq i \leq s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n,i)$ and also introduce events $B_i = \{\tilde{R}_n(I_n,i) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-i) \leq \tilde{R}_n(I_n,J_n^\star) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-J_n^\star)\}$ . Then, since $\tilde{R}_n(I_n,J_n) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-J_n) \leq \tilde{R}_n(I_n,J_n^\star) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-J_n^\star)$ is always true, we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge A_r\right]$$ (57) $$\leq \sum_{i=1}^{r} \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge B_i \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{58}$$ Observe that if $B_i$ is true, then at least one of the following events is also true. $$D_{1,i} = \{ \tilde{R}_n(I_n, i) \le R_n(h_{I_n,i}) - \varepsilon - c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n} - i) \},$$ (59) $$D_{2,i} = \{ R_n(h_{I_n,i}) < v(I_n, J_n^*) + 2\varepsilon \}, \tag{60}$$ $$D_3 = \{ \tilde{R}_n(I_n, J_n^*) > R_n(h_{I_n, J_n^*}) + \varepsilon + c_{I_n, \delta}(s_{I_n, n} - J_n^*) \}.$$ (61) From this we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge B_i \wedge A_r\right] \tag{62}$$ $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^*) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_{1,i} \wedge A_r\right] \tag{63}$$ $$+ \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_{2,i} \wedge A_r\right] \tag{64}$$ $$+ \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_3 \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{65}$$ First, notice that $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^*) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_{2,i} \wedge A_r\right] = 0. \tag{66}$$ Moreover, since $$\left| R_n(h_{I_n,i}) - \bar{R}(I_n,i) \right| \le 2\varepsilon \tag{67}$$ for $\bar{R}(j,i) = \frac{1}{s_{j,n}-i} \sum_{s=i+1}^{s_{j,n}} R_{t_{j,s}-1}(h_{j,i})$ we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[D_{1,i} \wedge A_r\right] \tag{68}$$ $$= \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(I_n, i) \le R_n(h_{I_n, i}) - 2\varepsilon - c_{I_n, \delta}(s_{I_n, n} - i) \land A_r\right]$$ (69) $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(I_n, i) \leq \bar{R}(I_n, i) - c_{I_n, \delta}(s_{I_n, n} - i) \wedge A_r\right]$$ (70) $$\leq \sum_{j \in \text{supp}(I_n)} \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(j,i) \leq \bar{R}(j,i) - c_{j,\delta}(s_{j,n} - i) \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{71}$$ From Lemma 3 we get that $$\mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(j,i) \le \bar{R}(j,i) - c_{j,\delta}(s_{j,n} - i) \wedge A_r\right]$$ (72) $$\leq \frac{\delta}{r^3(r+1)}. (73)$$ And, hence, $$\mathbb{P}\left[D_{1,i} \wedge A_r\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{r^3(r+1)}.\tag{74}$$ Similarly, $$\mathbb{P}\left[D_3 \wedge A_r\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{r^2(r+1)}.\tag{75}$$ Combining these two together, we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge A_r\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{r^2},\tag{76}$$ which gives us the statement on the lemma. *Proof of Theorem 3.* From Lemma [4](#page-13-0) we get that with high probability $$R_n(h_n) \le \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon. \tag{77}$$ Hence, we focus on bounding min1≤i≤sIn,n v(In, i). Observe that $$\min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) \tag{78}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} (R_n(h_{I_n,i}) + c_{I_n,\delta/2}(s_{I_n,n} - i)) \tag{79}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} (R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) + 2\varepsilon + c_{I_n,\delta/2}(s_{I_n,n}-i))$$ (80) $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) + 2\varepsilon \tag{81}$$ $$+2\sqrt{\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\log\frac{s_{I_n,n}^3(s_{I_n,n}+1)}{\delta}}.$$ (82) Similarly to Lemma [3,](#page-10-0) we have that with high probability on Ek,m: $$\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i})$$ (83) $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h_{I_n,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}) + \sqrt{\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \log \frac{s_{I_n,n}^2}{\delta}}.$$ (84) Similarly to the proof of Theorem 2, we get with high probability on Ek,m: $$-\inf_{h\in\mathcal{H}} R_n(h) \le -\inf_{h} \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})$$ (85) $$+\sqrt{\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\log\frac{s_{I_n,n}^2\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}),\beta,n)}{\delta}} + 4\beta.$$ (86) Therefore, we can conclude that $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) > W_{I_n,n} + U_{\delta}(I_n, \beta) \wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ (87) $$\leq \frac{3k\delta}{m} + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^c\right]. \tag{88}$$ Full learnability. First, we fix a sequence, εn, that converges to zero. Then, at a step n of MACRO, when it decides on the subroutines to update and chooses the active hypothesis, we perform all the necessary checks using the corresponding element of the sequence, i.e. εn. This change results in the following version of Theorem 2 (and analogous results can be derived from Theorems 1 and 3). Theorem 4. *For a convex loss* `*, if the subroutines of the meta-algorithm use an averaging for online-to-batch conversion, we have for any* α ∈ [0, 1] *and* β ∈ [0, α/8] $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_{n}(h_{n}) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_{n}(h) > \alpha + \frac{W_{I_{n},n}}{s_{I_{n},n}} + \frac{4}{s_{I_{n},n}} \sum_{t \in C_{I_{n},n}} \varepsilon_{t}\right]$$ $$\leq \frac{4k\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}), \beta, n)}{(\alpha/2 - 4\beta)^{2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}m(\alpha/2 - 4\beta)^{2}} + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^{c}\right].$$ (89) ![](_page_16_Figure_0.jpeg) Figure 4: Performance of MACRO with different subroutines on the DataExpo Airline dataset with the label-based distance function. Each row of plots corresponds to a different airport labeled by its IATA code. The y-axes shows error-rates in percents and the x-axes is labeled by the short name of a subroutine and a threshold used in MACRO. ERM-FTL, VW-EWA, ERM-FTL and VW-EWA represent the online strategies to choose the threshold. ERM-SR and VW-SR are the marginal versions of the subroutine algorithms and act as a baselines. The same conditions as above ensure the learnability for this modified version of the meta-algorithm. The described change also affects the number of subroutines started by MACRO. Following the same argument as in Lemma 1, this number is bounded by $\mathcal{N}(M,n,\varepsilon_n/2)$ , thereby, implying a trade-off between the accuracy of the approximations and the number of subroutines, which is equivalent to the amount of computations we do at each turn. *Proof of Theorem 4.* The only part that changes in the proof is the first step when we approximate the risk at step n by the average of risks. Then, instead of $2\varepsilon$ we get $2\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}\varepsilon_{t_{I_n,i}}$ . **Experiments** The way we compute distances between histories differ between the two studied settings. For the Airports dataset, since the number of flights in each time stamp changes, we compute the feature-based distance as the approximate bottleneck distance: to compare to sets of vectors, S and T, we compute all pairwise $\ell_2$ -distances between the elements of S and T, take the smallest $\max\{|S|\,,|T|\}$ ones and compute their average. Denoting this approximate bottleneck distance as $\bar{D}_1$ , the final distance between chunks is computed as $$D_1(S,T) = \frac{1}{2}\bar{D}_1(S^0, T^0) + \frac{1}{2}\bar{D}_1(S^1, T^1), \tag{90}$$ where $S^y$ and $T^y$ are the subsets of S and T with label y. For the Breakfast dataset, we fix a finite length history and compute the $\ell_2$ -distance between the vectors on the same positions and take the average. In the experiments, we used the distance of length 5, however, we tried out over values and found that the results are not very sensitive to the actual length. For the second distance, $D_2$ , we only make use of the labels of the points in the histories. For any history S, define $p(S) = (p_1, \dots, p_K)^T$ with $p_i$ being the fraction of the class i in S and K being the number of classes. Then we set $D_2(S,T) = \|p(S) - p(T)\|^2$ . The theoretical analysis is oblivious to the fact how we initialize the subroutines. In experiments, whenever we start a new subroutine, we give it a warm start by initializing it with the parameters of the closest subroutine in terms of discrepancies. The results for MACRO with label-based distance on the Airports dataset are presented in Figure 4 and on the Breakfast dataset are in Figure 5. In addition to the plots, we proide the exact error-rates for all the presented experiments in Tables 1, 2, 3 and 4. ![](_page_17_Figure_0.jpeg) Figure 5: Performance of MACRO with different subroutines on the Breakfast Actions dataset with the label-based distance function. Each plot corresponds to different action. The y-axes shows error-rates in percents averaged over the persons performing each action. [Question] What does the paper identify as the relationship between covering numbers and the fat-shattering dimension, and how does this relationship influence the control of the growth of covering numbers?

The paper identifies a relationship between covering numbers and the fat-shattering dimension through Lemma 2 (Corollary 1 of [24]). It states that for any function class \(\mathcal{F} \subseteq \{f : \mathcal{Z} \to [-1,1]\}\), the covering number \(\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{F}, \theta, n)\) is bounded by:

\[
\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{F}, \theta, n) \le \left(\frac{2en}{\theta}\right)^{fat_{\theta}(\mathcal{F})}.
\]

This relationship implies that the growth of covering numbers can be controlled by the fat-shattering dimension. Specifically, a larger fat-shattering dimension leads to a potentially larger covering number, while a smaller fat-shattering dimension can help keep the covering number in check. Thus, understanding the fat-shattering dimension provides a means to manage the complexity and growth of covering numbers in the context of function classes.

Now we can define covering numbers. **Definition 5.** A set, V, of $\mathbb{R}$ -valued trees of depth n is a (sequential) $\theta$ -cover (with respect to the $\ell_{\infty}$ -norm) of $\mathcal{F} \subset \{f: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathbb{R}\}$ on a tree $\mathbf{z}$ of depth n if $$\forall f \in \mathcal{F}, \forall \varepsilon \in \{\pm 1\}^n, \exists v \in V : \tag{12}$$ $$\max_{1 \le t \le n} |f(\mathbf{z}_t(\varepsilon)) - v_t(\varepsilon)| \le \theta. \tag{13}$$ The (sequential) $\theta$ -covering number of a function class $\mathcal{F}$ on a given tree $\mathbf{z}$ is $$\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{F}, \theta, \mathbf{z}) = \min\{ |V| : V \text{ is an } \theta\text{-cover}$$ (14) w.r.t. $$\ell_{\infty}$$ -norm of $\mathcal{F}$ on $\mathbf{z}$ \}. (15) The maximal $\theta$ -covering number of a function class $\mathcal{F}$ over depth-n trees is $$\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{F}, \theta, n) = \sup_{\mathbf{z}} \mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{F}, \theta, \mathbf{z}). \tag{16}$$ To control the growth of covering numbers we use the following notion of complexity. **Definition 6.** A $\mathcal{Z}$ -valued tree $\mathbf{z}$ of depth n is $\theta$ -shattered by a function class $\mathcal{F} \subseteq \{f : \mathcal{Z} \to \mathbb{R}\}$ if there exists an $\mathbb{R}$ -valued tree s of depth n such that $$\forall \varepsilon \in \{\pm 1\}^n, \exists f \in \mathcal{F} \text{ s.t. } 1 \le t \le n, \tag{17}$$ $$\varepsilon_t(f(\mathbf{z}_t(\varepsilon)) - s_t(\varepsilon)) > \theta/2.$$ (18) The (sequential) fat-shattering dimension $fat_{\theta}(\mathcal{F})$ at scale $\theta$ is the largest d such that $\mathcal{F}$ $\theta$ -shatters a $\mathcal{Z}$ -valued tree of depth d. An important result of [24] is the following connection between the covering numbers and the fat-shattering dimension. **Lemma 2** (Corollary 1 of [24]). Let $\mathcal{F} \subseteq \{f : \mathcal{Z} \to [-1,1]\}$ . For any $\theta > 0$ and any $n \ge 1$ , we have that $$\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{F}, \theta, n) \le \left(\frac{2en}{\theta}\right)^{fat_{\theta}(\mathcal{F})}.$$ (19) In all of the proofs we use the following technical lemma about the meta-algorithm. **Lemma 3.** Irrespectively of the subroutine used by the meta-algorithm, for any $\alpha \in [0,1]$ and $\beta \in [0,\beta/4]$ , we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[\sup_{h\in\mathcal{H}}\left|\frac{1}{s_{I_{n},n}}\sum_{t\in C_{I_{n},n}}(\ell(h,\mathbf{z}_{t})-R_{t-1}(h))\right|>\alpha\wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ $$\leq \frac{2k\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}),\beta,n)}{(\alpha-4\beta)^{2}}e^{-\frac{1}{2}m(\alpha-4\beta)^{2}}.$$ (20) Moreover, for any $\alpha \in [0,1]$ with $g_{I_n,i} = \ell(h_{I_n,i-1}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}) - R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i-1})$ $$\mathbb{P}\left[\left|\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}g_{I_n,i}\right|>\alpha\wedge E_{k,m}\right]\leq \frac{2k}{\alpha^2}e^{-\frac{1}{2}m\alpha^2}.$$ *Proof.* Introduce events $A_i = \{I_n = i\}$ for $i = 1, \ldots, k$ and $B_{i,j} = \{s_{j,n} = i\}$ (we suppress the dependence on n to increase readability). Observe that $E_{k,m} = \{\cup_{i \geq 1} A_i\} \land \{\cup_{i \geq m} B_{i,I_n}\}$ . Denoting $\Lambda(j) = \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\frac{1}{s_{i,n}} \sum_{t \in C_{i,n}} (\ell(h, \mathbf{z}_t) - R_{t-1}(h))|$ , we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(I_n) > \alpha \wedge E_{k,m}\right] \tag{21}$$ $$\leq \sum_{j \in \text{supp}(I_n)} \mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge \left\{ \cup_{i \geq m} B_{i,j} \right\} \right]. \tag{22}$$ Each of the last probabilities can be bounded using a union bound. $$\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge \{\cup_{i \ge m} B_{i,j}\}\right] \le \sum_{i > m} \mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge B_{i,j}\right]. \tag{23}$$ Using Lemma 4 from [33], we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge B_{i,j}\right] \le 2\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}), \beta, n)e^{-\frac{1}{2}i(\alpha - 4\beta)^{2}}.$$ (24) Summing the probabilities, we obtain the first statement of the lemma. For the second part of the lemma, denote $\Lambda(j) = |\frac{1}{s_{j,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{j,n}} (\ell(h_{j,i-1},\mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i}}) - R_{t_{j,i}-1}(h_{j,i-1}))|$ and, using the same decomposition as above, we have to bound $\mathbb{P}\left[\Lambda(j) > \alpha \wedge B_{i,j}\right]$ . Observe that each $h_{j,i}$ is adapted to the filtration generated by $\{\mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i}}\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ , hence, $\ell(h_{j,i},\mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i-1}}) - R_{t_{j,i-1}}(h_{j,i-1})$ behaves like a martingale difference sequence. However, there is a technical difficulty in the fact that the indices $t_{j,i}$ are in fact stopping times. To get around it, observe that we can write $\Lambda(j)$ as a sum over all the data with the adapted weights. Set $w_t$ to 1 if we updated the algorithm j at step t and to 0 otherwise. Correspondingly, define $h_t$ as the last chosen hypothesis by the j-th algorithm. This way, both $w_t$ and $h_t$ are adapted to the original process. Then $$\sum_{i=1}^{s_{j,n}} (\ell(h_{j,i-1}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{j,i}}) - R_{t_{j,i}-1}(h_{j,i-1}))$$ (25) $$= \sum_{t=1}^{n} w_t (\ell(\bar{h}_t, \mathbf{z}_t) - R_{t-1}(\bar{h}_t)).$$ (26) At this point we can again use Lemma 4 from [33] and get the second statement of the lemma. $\Box$ *Proof of Lemma 1.* The lower bound comes from the fact that MACRO constructs an $\varepsilon$ -covering. For the upper bound, observe that a new subroutine is started if and only if its associated conditional distribution differs by more than $\varepsilon$ from the ones of all previously created subroutines. Therefore, the set of conditional distribution associated with subroutines form an $\varepsilon$ -separated set with respect to $M_{i,j}$ 's (no two elements are closer than $\varepsilon$ to each other). The maximal size of such a set is at most the *covering number* of half the distance. *Proof of Theorem 1.* We start by the usual argument for the empirical risk minimization that allows us to focus on the uniform deviations. $$R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) \le 2 \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |R_n(h) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)|. \tag{27}$$ Denoting by R¯ n(h, In) = 1 sIn,n P t∈CIn,n Rt−1(h), we can upper bound the last term. $$\sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |R_n(h) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{28}$$ $$\leq \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |R_n(h) - \bar{R}_n(h, I_n)| + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{29}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i \in C_{I_n,n}} d_{i,n+1} + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{30}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i \in C_{I_n,n}} M_{i,n+1} + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)| \tag{31}$$ $$\leq 2\varepsilon + \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |\bar{R}_n(h, I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h, I_n)|, \tag{32}$$ where the last bound follows from the way the meta-algorithm chooses In. Hence, we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) > \alpha + 2\varepsilon\right]$$ (33) $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[\sup_{h\in\mathcal{H}}|\bar{R}_n(h,I_n) - \hat{R}_n(h,I_n)| > \alpha\right]. \tag{34}$$ The last probability can be bounded using Lemma [3](#page-10-0) giving us the statement of the theorem. *Proof of Theorem 2.* Note that by the way In is chosen, we get for any h ∈ H that $$R_n(h) - R_{t_{I_n,i-1}}(h) \le 2\varepsilon. \tag{35}$$ Therefore, by using the convexity of the loss $$R_n(h_n) \le \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_n(h_{I_n,i})$$ (36) $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) + 2\varepsilon. \tag{37}$$ Similarly, for any fixed h $$R_n(h) \ge \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h) - 2\varepsilon.$$ (38) Therefore, $$R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) \tag{39}$$ $$\leq 4\varepsilon + \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i})$$ (40) $$-\inf_{h\in\mathcal{H}}\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h). \tag{41}$$ We split the last difference into the following three terms and deal with them separately. $$T_1 = \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} (R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) - \ell(h_{I_n,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}))$$ (42) $$T_2 = \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h_{I_n,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})$$ (43) $$T_{3} = \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_{n},n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_{n},i}}) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{s_{I_{n},n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_{n},n}} R_{t_{I_{n},i}-1}(h).$$ (44) The first term can be bounded using Lemma 3. $T_2$ is in fact just $W_{I_n,n}$ . For $T_3$ observe that $$\inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h) \ge \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})$$ (45) $$+\inf_{h\in\mathcal{H}}\left(\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}(R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h)-\ell(h,\mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})).$$ (46) Therefore, $T_3$ is bounded by $\tilde{T}_3$ : $$\tilde{T}_{3} = \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \left( \frac{1}{s_{I_{n},n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_{n},n}} (\ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_{n},i}}) - R_{t_{I_{n},i}-1}(h)).$$ (47) Combining everything together, $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) - \inf_h R_n(h) > \alpha + 4\varepsilon + W_{I_n,n}\right]$$ (48) $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[T_1 + \tilde{T}_3 > \alpha \wedge E_{k,m}\right] + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^c\right] \tag{49}$$ $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[T_1 > \alpha/2 \wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ $$+ \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{T}_3 > \alpha/2 \wedge E_{k,m}\right] + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^c\right]$$ (50) The both terms in the last line can be bounded using Lemma 3 giving us the statement of the theorem. $\Box$ Online-to-batch conversion for non-convex losses. Here we describe the modification of the online-to-batch conversion method of [9]. As the original method was designed to work for i.i.d. data, we need to extend it to stochastic processes. The general idea is to assign a score to each of $h_{j,i}$ and choose the one with the lowest score. For a given confidence $\delta > 0$ , the score of $h_{j,i}$ is computed as $$u_n(j,i) = \tilde{R}_n(j,i) + c_{j,\delta}(s_{j,n} - i).$$ (51) where $$\tilde{R}_n(j,i) = \frac{1}{s_{j,n} - i} \sum_{k=i+1}^{s_{j,n}} \ell(h_{j,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{j,k}}) \quad \text{and} \quad c_{j,\delta}(t) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2(t+1)} \log \frac{s_{j,n}^3(s_{j,n}+1)}{\delta}}. \quad (52)$$ Setting $J_n = \operatorname{argmin}_{1 \leq i \leq s_{I_n,n}} u_n(I_n, i)$ , MACRO's output is $h_n = h_{I_n, J_n}$ . The following lemma is analog of Lemma 3 from [9] proved for the case of dependent data and the conditional risk. **Lemma 4.** For the setting of Theorem 3, let $$v(j,i) = R_n(h_{j,i}) + 2c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n} - i).$$ (53) Then we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge E_{k,m}\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{m}.$$ (54) *Proof.* Introduce events $A_r = \{|\sup(I_n)| \le k \land s_{I_n,n} = r\}$ . Using a union bound, we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ (55) $$\leq \sum_{r>m} \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \leq i \leq s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{56}$$ Therefore, we will focus on the last probabilities. Let $J_n^\star = \operatorname{argmin}_{1 \leq i \leq s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n,i)$ and also introduce events $B_i = \{\tilde{R}_n(I_n,i) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-i) \leq \tilde{R}_n(I_n,J_n^\star) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-J_n^\star)\}$ . Then, since $\tilde{R}_n(I_n,J_n) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-J_n) \leq \tilde{R}_n(I_n,J_n^\star) + c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n}-J_n^\star)$ is always true, we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge A_r\right]$$ (57) $$\leq \sum_{i=1}^{r} \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge B_i \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{58}$$ Observe that if $B_i$ is true, then at least one of the following events is also true. $$D_{1,i} = \{ \tilde{R}_n(I_n, i) \le R_n(h_{I_n,i}) - \varepsilon - c_{I_n,\delta}(s_{I_n,n} - i) \},$$ (59) $$D_{2,i} = \{ R_n(h_{I_n,i}) < v(I_n, J_n^*) + 2\varepsilon \}, \tag{60}$$ $$D_3 = \{ \tilde{R}_n(I_n, J_n^*) > R_n(h_{I_n, J_n^*}) + \varepsilon + c_{I_n, \delta}(s_{I_n, n} - J_n^*) \}.$$ (61) From this we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge B_i \wedge A_r\right] \tag{62}$$ $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^*) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_{1,i} \wedge A_r\right] \tag{63}$$ $$+ \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_{2,i} \wedge A_r\right] \tag{64}$$ $$+ \mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^{\star}) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_3 \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{65}$$ First, notice that $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_{I_n,i}) > v(I_n, J_n^*) + 2\varepsilon \wedge D_{2,i} \wedge A_r\right] = 0. \tag{66}$$ Moreover, since $$\left| R_n(h_{I_n,i}) - \bar{R}(I_n,i) \right| \le 2\varepsilon \tag{67}$$ for $\bar{R}(j,i) = \frac{1}{s_{j,n}-i} \sum_{s=i+1}^{s_{j,n}} R_{t_{j,s}-1}(h_{j,i})$ we have $$\mathbb{P}\left[D_{1,i} \wedge A_r\right] \tag{68}$$ $$= \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(I_n, i) \le R_n(h_{I_n, i}) - 2\varepsilon - c_{I_n, \delta}(s_{I_n, n} - i) \land A_r\right]$$ (69) $$\leq \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(I_n, i) \leq \bar{R}(I_n, i) - c_{I_n, \delta}(s_{I_n, n} - i) \wedge A_r\right]$$ (70) $$\leq \sum_{j \in \text{supp}(I_n)} \mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(j,i) \leq \bar{R}(j,i) - c_{j,\delta}(s_{j,n} - i) \wedge A_r\right]. \tag{71}$$ From Lemma 3 we get that $$\mathbb{P}\left[\tilde{R}_n(j,i) \le \bar{R}(j,i) - c_{j,\delta}(s_{j,n} - i) \wedge A_r\right]$$ (72) $$\leq \frac{\delta}{r^3(r+1)}. (73)$$ And, hence, $$\mathbb{P}\left[D_{1,i} \wedge A_r\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{r^3(r+1)}.\tag{74}$$ Similarly, $$\mathbb{P}\left[D_3 \wedge A_r\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{r^2(r+1)}.\tag{75}$$ Combining these two together, we get $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) > \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon \wedge A_r\right] \le \frac{k\delta}{r^2},\tag{76}$$ which gives us the statement on the lemma. *Proof of Theorem 3.* From Lemma [4](#page-13-0) we get that with high probability $$R_n(h_n) \le \min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) + 2\varepsilon. \tag{77}$$ Hence, we focus on bounding min1≤i≤sIn,n v(In, i). Observe that $$\min_{1 \le i \le s_{I_n,n}} v(I_n, i) \tag{78}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} (R_n(h_{I_n,i}) + c_{I_n,\delta/2}(s_{I_n,n} - i)) \tag{79}$$ $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} (R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) + 2\varepsilon + c_{I_n,\delta/2}(s_{I_n,n}-i))$$ (80) $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i}) + 2\varepsilon \tag{81}$$ $$+2\sqrt{\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\log\frac{s_{I_n,n}^3(s_{I_n,n}+1)}{\delta}}.$$ (82) Similarly to Lemma [3,](#page-10-0) we have that with high probability on Ek,m: $$\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} R_{t_{I_n,i}-1}(h_{I_n,i})$$ (83) $$\leq \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h_{I_n,i}, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}}) + \sqrt{\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \log \frac{s_{I_n,n}^2}{\delta}}.$$ (84) Similarly to the proof of Theorem 2, we get with high probability on Ek,m: $$-\inf_{h\in\mathcal{H}} R_n(h) \le -\inf_{h} \frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}} \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{t_{I_n,i}})$$ (85) $$+\sqrt{\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\log\frac{s_{I_n,n}^2\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}),\beta,n)}{\delta}} + 4\beta.$$ (86) Therefore, we can conclude that $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) > W_{I_n,n} + U_{\delta}(I_n, \beta) \wedge E_{k,m}\right]$$ (87) $$\leq \frac{3k\delta}{m} + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^c\right]. \tag{88}$$ Full learnability. First, we fix a sequence, εn, that converges to zero. Then, at a step n of MACRO, when it decides on the subroutines to update and chooses the active hypothesis, we perform all the necessary checks using the corresponding element of the sequence, i.e. εn. This change results in the following version of Theorem 2 (and analogous results can be derived from Theorems 1 and 3). Theorem 4. *For a convex loss* `*, if the subroutines of the meta-algorithm use an averaging for online-to-batch conversion, we have for any* α ∈ [0, 1] *and* β ∈ [0, α/8] $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_{n}(h_{n}) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_{n}(h) > \alpha + \frac{W_{I_{n},n}}{s_{I_{n},n}} + \frac{4}{s_{I_{n},n}} \sum_{t \in C_{I_{n},n}} \varepsilon_{t}\right]$$ $$\leq \frac{4k\mathcal{N}_{\infty}(\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}), \beta, n)}{(\alpha/2 - 4\beta)^{2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}m(\alpha/2 - 4\beta)^{2}} + \mathbb{P}\left[E_{k,m}^{c}\right].$$ (89) ![](_page_16_Figure_0.jpeg) Figure 4: Performance of MACRO with different subroutines on the DataExpo Airline dataset with the label-based distance function. Each row of plots corresponds to a different airport labeled by its IATA code. The y-axes shows error-rates in percents and the x-axes is labeled by the short name of a subroutine and a threshold used in MACRO. ERM-FTL, VW-EWA, ERM-FTL and VW-EWA represent the online strategies to choose the threshold. ERM-SR and VW-SR are the marginal versions of the subroutine algorithms and act as a baselines. The same conditions as above ensure the learnability for this modified version of the meta-algorithm. The described change also affects the number of subroutines started by MACRO. Following the same argument as in Lemma 1, this number is bounded by $\mathcal{N}(M,n,\varepsilon_n/2)$ , thereby, implying a trade-off between the accuracy of the approximations and the number of subroutines, which is equivalent to the amount of computations we do at each turn. *Proof of Theorem 4.* The only part that changes in the proof is the first step when we approximate the risk at step n by the average of risks. Then, instead of $2\varepsilon$ we get $2\frac{1}{s_{I_n,n}}\sum_{i=1}^{s_{I_n,n}}\varepsilon_{t_{I_n,i}}$ . **Experiments** The way we compute distances between histories differ between the two studied settings. For the Airports dataset, since the number of flights in each time stamp changes, we compute the feature-based distance as the approximate bottleneck distance: to compare to sets of vectors, S and T, we compute all pairwise $\ell_2$ -distances between the elements of S and T, take the smallest $\max\{|S|\,,|T|\}$ ones and compute their average. Denoting this approximate bottleneck distance as $\bar{D}_1$ , the final distance between chunks is computed as $$D_1(S,T) = \frac{1}{2}\bar{D}_1(S^0, T^0) + \frac{1}{2}\bar{D}_1(S^1, T^1), \tag{90}$$ where $S^y$ and $T^y$ are the subsets of S and T with label y. For the Breakfast dataset, we fix a finite length history and compute the $\ell_2$ -distance between the vectors on the same positions and take the average. In the experiments, we used the distance of length 5, however, we tried out over values and found that the results are not very sensitive to the actual length. For the second distance, $D_2$ , we only make use of the labels of the points in the histories. For any history S, define $p(S) = (p_1, \dots, p_K)^T$ with $p_i$ being the fraction of the class i in S and K being the number of classes. Then we set $D_2(S,T) = \|p(S) - p(T)\|^2$ . The theoretical analysis is oblivious to the fact how we initialize the subroutines. In experiments, whenever we start a new subroutine, we give it a warm start by initializing it with the parameters of the closest subroutine in terms of discrepancies. The results for MACRO with label-based distance on the Airports dataset are presented in Figure 4 and on the Breakfast dataset are in Figure 5. [Chunk 2] Conditional risk minimization (CRM) is a fundamental learning problem when the available data is not an i.i.d. sample from a fixed data distribution, but a sequence of interdependent observations, i.e. a *stochastic process*. Like i.i.d. samples, stochastic processes can be interpreted in a generative way: each data point is sampled from a *conditional data distribution*, where the conditioning is on the sequence of observations so far. This view is common, e.g., in the literature on time-series prediction, where the goal is to predict the next value of a time-series given the sequence of observed values to far. CRM is the discriminative analog to this: for a given loss function and a set of hypotheses, the goal is to identify the best hypothesis to apply at the next time step. Conditional risk minimization has many application for learning tasks in which data arrives sequentially and decisions have to be made quickly, e.g. frame-wise classification of video streams. As a more elaborate example, imagine the problem of predicting which flights will be delayed over the next hour at an airport. Clearly, the observed data for this task has a temporal structure and exhibits strong dependencies. A prediction function that is adjusted to the current conditions of the airport will be very useful. It will not only make it possible to predict the delays of incoming flights in advance, but also to identify the possible parameters changes that would avoid delays, e.g. by rescheduling or rerouting aircrafts [\[25\]](#page-9-0). Despite its fundamental nature, CRM remains largely an unsolved problem in theory as well as in practice. One reason is that it is a far harder task than ordinary i.i.d. learning, because the underlying conditional distributions, and therefore the optimal hypotheses, change at every time step. Until recently, it was not even clear how to formalize *successful learning* in the CRM context, as there exists no single target hypothesis to which a sequential learning process could converge. [\[33\]](#page-9-1) was the first work to offer a handle on the problem by formalizing a notion of *learnability* in the CRM sense, see Section [2](#page-1-0) for a formal discussion. Unfortunately, their results are purely theoretical as the suggested procedure would require storing all observations of the stochastic process for all future time steps, which is clearly not possible in practice. In this work, we make three contributions. First, we generalize the notion of learnability from [\[33\]](#page-9-1) to a more practical approximate *-learnability*, resembling the classic *probably approximately correct* (PAC) framework [29]. Second, we introduce MACRO, a meta-algorithm for CRM, and prove that it achieves $\varepsilon$ -learnability under less restrictive assumptions than previous approaches. At last, we show that MACRO is *practical*, as it requires only training a set of elementary learning algorithms on different subsets of the available data, but not storing the complete sequence of observations, and report on practical experiments that highlight MACRO's straight-forward applicability to real-world problems. **Related work** The fundamentals of statistical learning theory were build originally based on the assumption of independent and identically distributed data [30], but very soon extensions to stochastic processes were suggested. When one is more interested in short-term behaviour of the process, it makes sense to focus on the *conditional risk*, where the expectation is taken with respect to the conditional distribution of the process, as it was argued in [23, 26]. Learnability, i.e. the ability to perform a risk minimization with respect to the conditional risk, was established for a number of particular classes of stochastic processes, such as i.i.d., exchangeable, mixing and some others, see [27, 23, 6, 21, 32]. Most of these works focus on the estimation of the conditional risk by the average of losses over the observed data so far. Conditional risk minimization was considered by [16] with a later extension in [17]. Without trying to achieve learnability, they consider the behaviour of the empirical risk minimization algorithm at each fixed time step by using a non-adaptive estimator. A general study of learnability was performed in [33] by emphasizing the role of pairwise discrepancies and the necessity to use an adaptive estimator. A number of related setting that utilize the notion of conditional risk and its variants have been studied in [15, 32, 31]. A related topic to conditional risk minimization is time series prediction. While, in general, time series methods can not be applied to CRM, both fields share a number of ideas. Traditional approaches to prediction include forecasting by fitting different parametric models to the data, such as ARMA or ARIMA, or using spectral methods, see, e.g., [7]. Alternative approaches include nonparametric prediction of time series [19, 20, 3], and prediction by statistical learning [2, 18]. Similar research problems are studied in the field of dynamical systems, when one tries to identify the underlying transformation that governs the transitions, see [22, 11, 8, 28]. #### 2 Conditional Risk Minimization We first introduce our main notations. We are given a sequence of observations $\{\mathbf{z}_t\}_{t=1}^n$ from a stochastic process taking values in some space $\mathcal{Z}$ . The most common option for $\mathcal{Z}$ would be a product space, $\mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{Y}$ , where $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{Y}$ are input and output sets, respectively, of a supervised prediction problem. Other choices are possible, though, e.g. for modeling unsupervised learning tasks. We write $\mathbf{z}_{i:j}$ as a shorthand for a sequence $(\mathbf{z}_i, \dots, \mathbf{z}_j)$ for $i \leq j$ . We fix a hypotheses class $\mathcal{H}$ , which is usually a subset of $\{h : \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{D}\}$ with $\mathcal{D}$ being some decision space, e.g. $\mathbb{R}^d$ . We also fix a loss function $\ell : \mathcal{D} \times \mathcal{Z} \to [0,1]$ , that allows us to evaluate the loss of a given hypothesis h on a given point z as $\ell(h(z),z) = \ell(h,z)$ , with the latter version used to shorten the notations. For any time step t, we denote by $\mathbb{E}_t[f(\mathbf{z}_{t+1})]$ the expectation of a function f with respect to the distribution of the next step, conditioned on the data so far, i.e. $\mathbb{E}[f(\mathbf{z}_{t+1})|\mathbf{z}_{1:t}]$ To characterize the capacity of the hypothesis class we use sequential covering numbers with respect to the $\ell_{\infty}$ -norm, $\mathcal{N}_{\infty}$ , introduced by [24]. This complexity measure is applied to the induced functions space $\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H}) = \{\ell(h,\cdot), \forall h \in \mathcal{H}\}$ . Throughout the paper we assume that $\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{H})$ has a finite sequential fat-shattering dimension, a notion of complexity of a function class. These quantities are generalizations of more traditional measures for i.i.d. data, so readers unfamiliar with the theory of stochastic processes can read the results in terms of usual covering numbers and VC dimension. The formal definitions can be found in the supplementary material. Our task is to find a hypothesis of minimal risk, i.e. solve $$\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h),\tag{1}$$ where $R_n(h) = \mathbb{E}_n \left[ \ell(h, \mathbf{z}_{n+1}) \right]$ is the conditional risk at step n. Note that the conditional distribution is different at every time step, so the objective also constantly changes. At each step we can compute only an approximate solution, $h_n$ , based on the data. A desired property is an improvement of the quality with the amount of the observations, as summarized in the following definition. **Definition 1** (Learnability [33]). For a fixed loss function $\ell$ and a hypotheses class $\mathcal{H}$ , we call a class of processes $\mathcal{C}$ conditionally learnable in the limit if there exists an algorithm that, for every process P in $\mathcal{C}$ , produces a sequence of hypotheses, $h_n$ , each based on $\mathbf{z}_{1:n}$ , satisfying $$R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) \to 0 \tag{2}$$ in probability over the samples drawn from P. An algorithm that satisfies (2) we call a limit learner for the class C. From a practical perspective, one might be satisfied with achieving only some target accuracy. For this purpose, we introduce the following relaxed definition. **Definition 2** ( $\varepsilon$ -Learnability). For a fixed loss function $\ell$ and a hypotheses class $\mathcal{H}$ , we call a class of processes $\mathcal{C}$ $\varepsilon$ -conditionally learnable for $\varepsilon > 0$ if there exists an algorithm that, for every process P in $\mathcal{C}$ , produces a sequence of hypotheses, $h_n$ , each based on $\mathbf{z}_{1:n}$ , satisfying $$\mathbb{P}\left[R_n(h_n) - \inf_{h \in \mathcal{H}} R_n(h) > \varepsilon\right] \to 0.$$ (3) An algorithm that satisfies (3) we call an $\varepsilon$ -learner for the class C. A class of processes is *learnable* in the sense of [33] if and only if it is $\varepsilon$ -learnable for all $\varepsilon > 0$ . #### 2.1 Discrepancies Following [33], our approach relies on a specific notion of distance between distributions called *discrepancy*, a subclass of integral probability metrics, [34]. It is a popular measure used, for example, in the field of domain adaptation [12, 5, 4]. In this work we use this distance only to quantify distances between the conditional distributions, therefore we define the discrepancies only between those. **Definition 3 (Pairwise discrepancy).** For a sample $\mathbf{z}_1, \mathbf{z}_2, \ldots$ from a fixed stochastic process, the pairwise discrepancy between time points i and j is $$d_{i,j} = \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} |R_{i-1}(h) - R_{j-1}(h)|.$$ (4) Given a sequence of conditional distributions, $P_t = \mathbb{P}\left[\,\cdot\,|\,\mathbf{z}_{1:t}\right]$ , an interesting quantity is the covering number of the space $\{P_0,\ldots,P_n\}$ with the discrepancy as a metric, which we denote by $\mathcal{N}(d,n,\varepsilon)$ . If, as a thought experiments, we identify $\varepsilon$ -close distributions with each other, then $\mathcal{N}(d,n,\varepsilon)$ characterizes the minimal number of distributions sufficient to represent all of them. This quantity can therefore serve as a measure of the complexity of learning from this sequence and it appears naturally in the analysis of the performance of our algorithm as a lower bound on the necessary computational resources. [Question] What challenges does conditional risk minimization (CRM) face in practical applications, and how does the proposed MACRO meta-algorithm address these challenges according to the introduction?

Conditional risk minimization (CRM) faces several challenges in practical applications, primarily due to the nature of the data being a sequence of interdependent observations rather than independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) samples. This results in the following difficulties:

1. **Changing Conditional Distributions**: The underlying conditional distributions and optimal hypotheses change at every time step, making it harder to formalize successful learning.
2. **Storage Requirements**: Previous approaches to CRM required storing all observations of the stochastic process for all future time steps, which is impractical.

The proposed MACRO meta-algorithm addresses these challenges by:

1. **Generalizing Learnability**: It introduces a more practical notion of approximate *-learnability*, resembling the classic *probably approximately correct* (PAC) framework.
2. **Reducing Storage Needs**: MACRO requires only training a set of elementary learning algorithms on different subsets of the available data, rather than storing the complete sequence of observations.
3. **Proving Practicality**: The algorithm is shown to achieve ε-learnability under less restrictive assumptions than previous methods, making it applicable to real-world problems where data arrives sequentially and decisions must be made quickly.

[Context] [Chunk 1] In this set of experiments we present MACRO in a quite different setting. We use the Breakfast Actions Dataset[2](#page-7-1) , which consists of videos of 52 people performing 10 actions related to breakfast preparation. Each combination of a person and an action is treated as a separate learning task and the performance is measured by per frame error rate. Following the usage of a Gaussianity assumption by previous approaches [\[13,](#page-8-17) [14\]](#page-8-18), we use Gaussian Naive Bayes classifiers trained online as subroutines. G-NB The algorithm tracks the running average in the feature space for each class separately and predicts the class with the closest mean. After receiving a new point, the algorithm incrementally updates the mean of the corresponding class. As for the airports dataset, we present the results for the feature-based distance, while results for the label-based one can be found in the supplement. As above, we also evaluate the FTL and EWA strategies for threshold selection. The results are presented in Figure [3.](#page-6-1) We observe that the effect of MACRO is even stronger than for the airport dataset. The error-rate is always reduced, in some cases by more than 70% relatively to the baseline. Both threshold-selection strategies show excellent performance, with FTL again outperforming EWA. Overall, we see that MACRO consistently outperforms the traditional online algorithms for both datasets. This illustrates two facts: CRM is indeed a promising approach to sequential prediction problems, and MACRO allows applying CRM principles to large real-world datasets that previously suggested methods are unable to handle.
